feed.title: Combined summary - Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR

entry.title: Combined summary - Aligning privacy incentives in P2MR
entry.summary: The recent discussions on the Bitcoin Improvement Proposal (BIP) 360 have brought to light significant considerations regarding the implementation of Merkle authentication paths in the control block of Pay-to-Migration-Request (P2MR) transactions. The proposed modification aims to prohibit depth-zero script trees within P2MR, which currently enable smaller witness sizes but compromise privacy and security. By enforcing a minimum requirement of a depth-1 tree, the incentive to use simpler, less private scripting methods would be eliminated, enhancing both the efficiency and privacy of Bitcoin scripting akin to that of Pay-to-Taproot (P2TR). Antoine, an active participant in these discussions, has raised concerns about the economic implications of adopting the P2MR strategy over P2TRv2, particularly in terms of transaction costs. He argues that while P2MR might offer long-range protection against potential threats from Cryptographically Relevant Quantum Computing (CRQC), it does so at the expense of higher transaction costs, which could deter users from migrating to this new system. His analysis suggests that a systemic migration is crucial and should not be hindered by cost barriers that discourage user participation. Antoine advocates for the adoption of P2TRv2, which is aligned more closely with user behaviors and incentives, citing its lower costs and better suitability for widespread adoption. Further discussions have highlighted slow adoption rates of new Bitcoin address formats like P2MR and P2TRv2. Historical trends indicate that even with incentives, new technologies are adopted slowly within the Bitcoin ecosystem. For instance, seven years after its introduction, the P2TR format still accounts for only a minimal fraction of the Unspent Transaction Output (UTXO) set and the total Bitcoin supply. This pattern underscores the modest impact that incentives have on accelerating the uptake of new technologies in this space. Reports from sources such as [mempool.space](https://www.mempool.space) and [Galaxy Digital](https://www.galaxydigital.com) support these observations, providing empirical evidence of the slow adoption rates. In anticipation of future cryptographic developments, the conversation has also ventured into the realm of post-quantum (PQ) cryptographic strategies within the Bitcoin network. The suggested approach involves initially deploying a PQ output type to attract a minority of UTXOs, with further actions planned in response to significant cryptographic events. This proactive strategy aims to safeguard the majority of UTXOs that have not migrated to PQ wallets, possibly coupled with disabling Elliptic Curve (EC) spending to prevent unauthorized access. Such preemptive measures could lead to community debates and potentially a fork, underscoring the importance of adequate preparation today to ensure the resilience of Bitcoin against future threats. Overall, these discussions reflect a deep engagement with both the theoretical and practical aspects of Bitcoin's future security and usability. The community continues to explore optimizations and enhancements, such as the proposed reduction in cost associated with single-signer Schnorr transactions by 32 bytes, indicating ongoing efforts to refine and improve the scalability and economic efficiency of blockchain technology.
